coredata.dc:title: Temperature response TADF/ RTP dual mode afterglow of carbon dots and flake C3N4 composites for information encryption and advanced lighting
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wenquan
Bao, Xin
Wang, Hui
Liu, Zixuan
Tian, Zhen
Li, Hui
Yuan, Xi
coredata.dc:description: TADF emission based on CD materials has inspired extensive attention in recent years. Herein, CD composites with afterglow color temperature-responsive were prepared by confining BCDs/ BPCDs in a g-C3N4 matrix. The covalent bonds extensively protect the triplet states and promote the ISC process, and the small △EST is sufficient to encourage the RISC process. At room temperature, the BCD composite exhibits a deep blue TADF emission, and the BPCD composite shows two TADF emission peaks in the blue-green region. BCD/ BPCD composites exhibit RTP emission at lower temperatures, and the competition between TADF and RTP leads to the color temperature-responsive properties in these composites. A simple concept of information encryption was developed to demonstrate the great potential of these composites in advanced security applications. Based on the high overall quantum yields (44 %) and broad full width at half maximum (over 140 nm) at the visible region of the BPCD composites, they are successfully applied to develop the single component fluorescence/ TADF dual-emission delay WLED with CIE coordinates and CRI of (0.32, 0.34) and 92. This work provides a new strategy for producing CDs-based TADF materials and may motivate CDs TADF materials applied in information encryption and advanced lighting application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Afterglow
Composite
Temperature-responsive
Delay lighting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphological and photophysical characterization of BCDs/ BPCDs
The photophysical characterization of BCD/ BPCD composites
The morphological characterization of BCD/ BPCD composites
Information encryption and single component delay WLED

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HR-TEM images of a) BCDs and b) BPCDs. Excitation-photoluminescence maps of c) BCDs aqueous solution and d) BPCDs aqueous solution.

A) absorption spectra of bcds aqueous solution, bpcds aqueous solution, bcd composite powder, and bpcd composite powder. b) Excitation-photoluminescence maps of BCD composite powder. c) Excitation-photoluminescence maps of BCD composite powder. d) Photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of BCD composites excited by 360 nm. e) Photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of BPCD composites excited at 360 nm. f) Afterglow decay curves of BCD composites (measured at 410 nm) and BPCD composites (measured at 425 nm and 508 nm) excited by 360 nm. The afterglow spectra were collected with a delay time of 0.5 ms. g) Corresponding photographs of BCD composite powders (top) and BPCD composite powders (bottom) under conditions of 365 nm UV lamp on and off.

Temperature-dependent photoluminescence spectra of a) BCD composites and d) BPCD composites. Temperature-dependent afterglow spectra of b) BCD composites and d) BPCD composites. Photoluminescence and afterglow spectra of c) BCD composites and f) BPCD composites excited by 360 nm at 77 K. The afterglow spectra were collected with a delay time of 0.5 ms.
A) sem image of bpcd composite. b) ftir spectra of bpcd composite. c) full xps spectra of bpcd composite. high-resolution d) c 1 s, e) N 1 s, and f) O 1 s XPS spectra of BPCD composite.
Proposed mechanism of the photophysical processes in BCD/BPCD composites at different temperatures.

A) digital photographs of information encryption based on bcd composites. b) luminescence spectrum of the wleds with ingan uv (365 nm). c) CIE coordinates of the BPCD composite-based WLED. d) Photographs of the BPCD composite-based delay WLED switched on and off.
The Schematic diagram shows the preparation process of BCD/BPCD composites.

head.ce:title: Temperature response TADF/ RTP dual mode afterglow of carbon dots and flake C3N4 composites for information encryption and advanced lighting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Key Laboratory of Solid-State Laser Technology and Application, School of Physics, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Key Laboratory of Solid-State Laser Technology and Application
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Key Laboratory of Solid-State Laser Technology and Application, School of Physics, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Aviation University of Air Force, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Aviation University of Air Force, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots composites with temperature-response TADF/ RTP dual mode afterglow emission were developed by embedding carbon dots in a g-C3N4 nanosheet matrix through a microwave heating process. Based on the ultra-long lifetimes, high QY, and broader emission bands of these composites, afterglow emission, information encryption, and delayed WLED were demonstrated.

TADF emission based on CD materials has inspired extensive attention in recent years. Herein, CD composites with afterglow color temperature-responsive were prepared by confining BCDs/ BPCDs in a g-C3N4 matrix. The covalent bonds extensively protect the triplet states and promote the ISC process, and the small △EST is sufficient to encourage the RISC process. At room temperature, the BCD composite exhibits a deep blue TADF emission, and the BPCD composite shows two TADF emission peaks in the blue-green region. BCD/ BPCD composites exhibit RTP emission at lower temperatures, and the competition between TADF and RTP leads to the color temperature-responsive properties in these composites. A simple concept of information encryption was developed to demonstrate the great potential of these composites in advanced security applications. Based on the high overall quantum yields (44 %) and broad full width at half maximum (over 140 nm) at the visible region of the BPCD composites, they are successfully applied to develop the single component fluorescence/ TADF dual-emission delay WLED with CIE coordinates and CRI of (0.32, 0.34) and 92. This work provides a new strategy for producing CDs-based TADF materials and may motivate CDs TADF materials applied in information encryption and advanced lighting application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow emission materials have set off a research upsurge in illumination, information encryption, biological imaging, sensing, and other fields due to their long lifetime and large Stokes shift. The afterglow photoluminescence originates from the excitons on the triplet states, which mainly contain room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and delayed fluorescence (DF)[1,2]. The excitons on the triplet states could return to the ground state through the radiative transition directly, which generates the RTP emission, or first return to the singlet states by reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) and then return to the ground state through radiative transition, which generate thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) emission[3–8]. Traditional afterglow materials (metal-containing inorganic coordination compounds and organic compounds) have suffered some drawbacks such as biological toxicity, high cost, and un-stability[9–11], the preparation of newly afterglow materials is desired.

As a class of emerging carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn considerable attention in bioimaging, illumination, photocatalysis, and sensor[12–17]. CD-based afterglow materials also acquired intense research in recent years[18–25]. Compared to traditional afterglow materials, they have stable photoluminescent properties, easy preparation, low cost, and environmental friendliness. TADF emission has the potential to harvest both triplet and singlet excitons that achieve a higher quantum efficiency, but most of the afterglow emission phenomenon based on CDs have reported is RTP emission up to now. The protection of the triplet state and a smaller energy gap (<0.30 eV) between singlet and triplet states (△EST) are necessary for the TADF emission[26]. It is verified that embedding CDs in some rigid matrix (such as silica, cyanuric acid, and boric acid) via covalent bonds could greatly protect the triplet state and decrease the △EST. For example, Yu’s groups have developed a series of CD-based TADF materials via a ‘‘dots-in-zeolite’’ strategy which embedded CDs in a zeolite matrix and realized the protection of triplet state[27–30]. After recent research, the CD-based TADF color has covered the visible range, but the deep-blue (<420 nm) TADF emission remains a significant challenge[31]. In addition, some CD composites with RTP/ TADF dual emission have appeared, exhibiting the afterglow color-tunable via temperature change[32,33]. Still, it is also challenging to realize multicolor TADF emission simultaneously in single-component CD materials.

Recently, fluorescence/ afterglow dual emission CD-based materials have aroused increasing attention in single-component delay WLED due to high efficiency, low cost, and eye protection[34–39]. The delay lighting also has the potential to produce alternating current LED. Shan’s group has prepared and applied a series of CDs afterglow materials to the lighting illumination[36,37]. Shen et al. prepared dual-mode white CDs with a record QY of 67.35 % and realized WLED with a CRI of 85[38]. However, most of these reports realize the delay WLED based on the fluorescence/ RTP dual emission. The mode of fluorescence/ TADF dual emission delay WLED based on CD materials is rare. In addition, most of these delayed WLEDs based on CD materials have been reported to exhibit a color rendering index lower than 90, which limits their application prospects. So it is significant to develop high CRI WLED based on single-component CDs fluorescence/ TADF dual emission materials.

Herein, we prepared two kinds of CDs-based composites with high-efficiency fluorescence/ afterglow emission through a one-point simple microwave treatment of BCDs/ BPCDs and urea. The CDs embedded in the flake g-C3N4 with small size by the covalent bonds generated through dehydration and carbonization reaction between the function groups in CDs surface and urea/ g-C3N4. This structure prevents aggregation-induced quenching in the solid state, dramatically protects the triplet state, and promotes the ISC process. The △EST in BCD composite is calculated as 0.28 eV, which is sufficiently small to realize the RISC process and a deep blue TADF emission at 410 nm with 270 ms lifetime was achieved at room temperature. Due to the dropping of the P element, the photoluminescent properties of BPCD composites have a noticeable change which exhibits blue-green dual color fluorescence/ afterglow emission with a high QY of 44.6 %, and the TADF emission at 508 nm showed long lifetime up to 648 ms at room temperature. RTP emission was also found in BCD/ BPCD composites, and the properties of temperature-responsive color-tunable afterglow emission were shown in these composites due to the competition between RTP and TADF at different temperatures. A simple concept of information encryption was developed based on the long-lived afterglow emission of BCD composites, demonstrating the great potential of BCD/ BPCD composites in advanced security applications. Based on the high QY and broadband dual-mode emission, we also realized single component time delay WLED by combining BPCD composites with UV-LED chips, which exhibit the properties of protecting eyes and high-efficiency white light emission with CIE coordinates of (0.32, 0.34) and 90.2 color rendering index (CRI).

Urea (99 %) and m-phenylenediamine (99 %) were purchased from Macklin (China), phosphoric acid and ethanol were purchased from Guang fu. Deionized water was purified by a purification system in our laboratory.

BCDs: BCDs were prepared by a solvothermal reaction, similar to the previous reports[15], 0.5 g m-phenylenediamine and 30 mL ethanol were used as the precursor materials. m-phenylenediamine dissolved in ethanol and stirred into a transparent solution, and then the solution was transferred to a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the reactor is naturally cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 8000 r min −1 for 10 min to remove the large particles. Finally, the liquid supernatant was heated at 150 °C in an oven until the BCDs powder was acquired.

BPCDs: The synthesis of BPCDs is similar to the BCDs. First, 0.5 g m-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 30 mL ethanol and stirred into a transparent solution. Then 2 mL phosphoric acid was added slowly, and the mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. After heating at 180 °C for 12 h, the dark solution was acquired and centrifuged at 8000 r min −1 for 10 min to remove the large particles. Finally, the liquid supernatant was heated at 150 °C in an oven until the BPCDs powder was acquired.

BCD/ BPCD composites were prepared through a one-step microwave-assisted heating: 0.05 g BCDs/ BPCDs powders and 5 g urea were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water, and then the mixed solution was heated by a 750 W microwave oven. After the microwave treatment for 195 s, we acquired the solid materials with bright fluorescence and afterglow emission, which could be grinded to the BCD/ BPCD composite powders. It is noteworthy that the BCD/ BPCD composites could also be prepared after a shorter or longer microwave heating time, but the most excellent photoluminescent properties in these composites were acquired in 195 s.

Blue CDs-based phosphor was prepared based on our report before[13] and applied in information encryption. Blue fluorescence CDs were synthesized from the precursors of citric acid and urea via a solvothermal reaction.
The CDs could be embedded in silica chains through a microwave-assisted method, effectively preventing aggregation. The blue CDs-based phosphors were acquired after ground the product into powders.

TEM images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope, and SEM images were obtained on a JSM 5600LV scanning electron microscope. FTIR spectra were obtained with a Perkin–Elmer spectrometer, XPS analysis was performed on a KRATOS Axis Ultra spectrometer using Al ka as the excitation source. XRD data were collected on a Siemens D5005 diffractometer. UV–vis absorption spectra of BCDs, BPCDs, and composites were collected from a Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrophotometer. Fluorescence excitation-emission maps, fluorescence spectra, afterglow spectra, and lifetime decay curves were measured using a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrometer with a phosphorescent system. PLQYs were calculated by Otsuka QE-2000 with semi integrating sphere. The emission spectra of LED were obtained using a labsphere optical spectrometer.

Time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves were collected under 360 nm excitation, and average luminescence lifetimes were calculated using the following equation (1):
τ
a v g
= ∑
a i
τ
i
2
/ ∑
a i
τ i
Time-resolved luminescence decay curves were fitted with tri-exponential functions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphological and photophysical characterization of BCDs/ BPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: BCDs and BPCDs were synthesized via a similar solvothermal reaction, and their morphological structures were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The BCDs and BPCDs particles both exhibited well-dispersed with an average diameter of 4–5 nm (Fig. 1 a and 1b), and high-resolution (HR-TEM) images reveal graphite (100) lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm (as shown in Figure S1). According to the atomic force microscopy (AFM) images (Figure S2), BCDs and BPCDs exhibit the heights of 3 nm approximately. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrometers of BCDs and BPCDs were measured to clarify the surface function groups and chemical structure. As shown in Figure S3a, the FTIR spectra of BCDs exhibit a broad absorption from 3000 to 3700 cm−1 owing to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (O–H), amino (N–H), and methylene (C–H). The absorption peaks located at 1603 cm−1 and 1495 cm−1 were attributable to the stretching vibration of C = N and the bending vibration of C-N. And for the FTIR spectra of BPCDs (Figure S3b), the new absorption peaks at 967 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of P-N and C-O/ P-O. The full XPS survey spectra (Figure S4a and d) revealed the atomic percentages of C, N, and O are 74.95 %, 18.91 %, and 6.4 % in BCDS, for the BPCDs the atomic percentages of C, N, O, and P are 50.66 %, 6.2 %, 36.65 %, and 6.49 %, respectively. High-resolution XPS C 1 s spectra of BCDs display three peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.5 eV, and 286.3 eV, which can be assigned to C–C/ C = C, C-N, and C-O bonds, respectively (Figure S4b). Comparing the XPS C 1 s spectra with BCDs, a pronounced increase of C-O and C-P bonds could be found in BPCDs (Figure S4e). The O 1 s HR-XPS spectra of BCDs (Figure S4c) exhibit a peak centered at 531.4 eV, which belongs to C-OH/ C-O-C bonds. BPCDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks at about 530.4 eV, 531.4 eV, and 532.1 eV, contributing to P = O, C-OH/ C-O-C, and P-O, respectively (Figure S4f). The results of FTIR and XPS indicate that the P element has doped in BPCDs successfully.

BCDs and BPCDs exhibit excellent properties of photoluminescence. The fluorescence excitation-emission maps of BCDs and BPCDs aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 1c and 1d. BCDs aqueous solution exhibits an emission peak centered at around 480 nm and with photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 47 % under 360 nm excitation. The BPCDs aqueous solution showed a similar emission center peaked at around 470 nm, but a stronger green emission peaked at 508 nm also could be observed. It could be illustrated that the dropping of the P elements changes the bandgap of CDs. Under 360 nm excited, the PLQY of the BPCDs aqueous solution was measured as 67.4 %. Although excellent photoluminescence properties were observed in an aqueous solution, BCDs/ BPCDs have not shown solid-state illumination and afterglow emission (Figure S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photophysical characterization of BCD/ BPCD composites
ce:section.ce:para: Then the photoluminescence and afterglow spectra delayed by 0.5 ms for BCD/ BPCD composite powders at room temperature were measured, shown in Fig. 2d and Fig. 2e (under 360 nm excitation). For the BCD composites, the photoluminescence and afterglow spectra both displayed an emission peak centered at 410 nm, and the slight difference between them could be deduced that the afterglow emission may be delayed fluorescence. A similar phenomenon could be found in the BPCD composite. The afterglow spectra displayed two emission peaks at 425 nm and 508 nm, which matched the photoluminescence spectra well. Based on the results of 3D maps and the photoluminescence/ afterglow spectra of BCD/ BPCD composites, we consider that dropping P elements significantly changes the band gap and forms a new green emission center in BPCDs. After embedding in the matrix BPCDs mainly determine the photophysical properties of these composite materials, so green fluorescence/ afterglow emission could be observed. Time-resolved afterglow decay curves of BCD/ BPCD composites (excited at 360 nm) were also shown in Fig. 2f, which could be fitted with a triexponential function. The average lifetime of BCD composite at 410 nm is 270 ms, and the BPCD composite powders, which have two afterglow emission peaks, were calculated as 250.2 ms (at 425 nm) and 648 ms (at 508 nm), respectively.

To further investigate the photoluminescence properties and mechanism of these composites, temperature-dependent fluorescence/ afterglow emission spectra (excited at 360 nm) of these composites were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 a, BCDs composite exhibit blue fluorescence emission centered at 410 nm under different temperatures. The fluorescence intensity has no obvious change from 80 K to 170 K and then a weaker enhancement with the temperature increasing from 170 K to 330 K (Fig. 3a). However, the afterglow emission of BCD composite exhibit the properties of temperature dependence. As shown in Fig. 3b, BCDs composite have a single phosphorescence emission at 450 nm under 80 K, and the intensity of this peak has no obvious change from 80 K to 150 K. However, when the temperature increased continuously (from 190 K to 330 K), the afterglow intensity in 450 nm gradually decreased (exhibit the properties of RTP emission). Interestingly that a new afterglow emission peaked at 410 nm appeared and enhanced with the temperature climbing from 80 K to 310 K. The △EST of BCD composite was calculated as 0.28 eV using the prompt and delayed spectra at 77 K (Fig. 3c). Based on the similar emission peak with the fluorescence, small energy gap, and the properties of intensity thermal enhancement, we consider the afterglow centered at 410 nm belongs to TADF emission. The peak intensity dropped gradually when the temperature increased from 310 K to 350 K. It could be illustrated by the enhancement of non-radiative transitions (such as molecular vibration) in high temperatures. In addition, a similar phenomenon could also be observed in BPCD composite powders which have two fluorescence peaks. With the temperature increasing from 80 K to 330 K, the blue and green fluorescence emission intensities are stable (Fig. 3d). Temperature-dependent afterglow spectra of the BPCD composite are shown in Fig. 3e. At 80 K, the sample exhibit a broader emission band peaked at 460 nm. The intensity of this peak has no obvious change from 80 K to 150 K. But, it gradually decreases when the temperature rises from 190 K. Based on the above properties, we also consider the afterglow that peaked at 460 nm belongs to the RTP emission. With the temperature increasing, two new afterglow emissions peaked at 425 nm and 508 nm appeared gradually, dramatically corresponding to the fluorescence emission peaks. The intensity of the two emission peaks has obvious enhancement with the temperature increasing, confirming they belong to TADF. The prompt and delayed spectra of the BPCD composite at 77 K are shown in Fig. 3f, and the delayed curve was deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 455 nm, 488 nm, and 512 nm, approximately (Figure S6a). To exclude the effect of matrix materials on the composite photoluminescence, the afterglow emission curves of BCD/ BPCD composites were also collected at 77 K. Only a weaker signal centered at 488 nm could be observed (as shown in Figure S6b) in matrix materials, which verified the photoluminescence properties of these composites were determined by BCDs/ BPCDs. Based on the results of these spectra, the afterglow emissions peaked at 455 nm and 512 nm were verified to belong to the BPCD composites, so the △EST between the singlet state and the triplet state of these two emission centers were calculated as 0.19 eV and 0.02 eV. The small △EST between singlet and triplet states in BCD/ BPCD composites further verified the RISC process, and TADF could be easily realized.

Because of the competition between RTP and TADF emission under different temperatures, BCD/ BPCD composites exhibit the properties of afterglow color temperature-response. To further explore the afterglow properties of these composites, temperature-dependent afterglow spectra excited at 254 nm were measured and shown in Figure S8. The afterglow of the BCD composite also displays a temperature-response deep blue emission, but for the BPCD composite, the green TADF was notably weak. The afterglow images of these composites at different excitations/ temperatures are shown in Figure S9. Based on the properties of afterglow color, temperature-sensitive and excitation-sensitive, they could be applied for information encryption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphological characterization of BCD/ BPCD composites
ce:section.ce:para: To further confirm the photoluminescence mechanism, the structure and surface states of BCD/BPCD composites were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), FTIR, XRD, and XPS spectra. Compared to the matrix materials, the content of BCDs and BPCDs dropped in composites is very rare, so the SEM image, FTIR, and XPS spectra between BCD/BPCD composites are nearly identical. As shown in the SEM image (Fig. 4a), these composites comprise the nanosheet structure. The energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) spectra and P elements mapping of BPCD composite were shown in Figure S10, further confirming the BPCDs combined with matrix material. FTIR spectra of the composite were shown in Fig. 4b, the broad absorption from 2500 to 3600 cm−1 owing to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (O–H), amino (N–H), and methylene (C–H). The characteristic peaks located at 1710 cm−1 were attributable to the typical stretching vibration of C = O. FTIR spectra of urea and the composites microwave heated for different times were shown in Figure S11, with the microwave heating time increasing the signals of triazine units (at about 800 cm−1) and the stretching and breathing mode of heterocycles between 1200 and 1600 cm−1 [40] enhanced gradually. The phenomenon could be illustrated that the urea translates to the carbon nitride under microwave irradiation. XPS spectra of these composites further indicated BPCD composite mainly consisted of 34.4 at% C, 38.6 at% N, 26.4 at% O, and 0.6 at% P, respectively. For the high-resolution XPS (HR-XPS) of C 1 s was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.1 eV, and 289.3 eV, which were attributed to C = C, C-N/ C = N, and C = O, respectively. The N 1 s HR-XPS spectra showed two peaks at 398.7 eV and 399.8 eV, corresponding to the pyridine N and N = C-N. And the O 1 s XPS band could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.9 eV and 533.9 eV for C = O and C-O. These results verified BCDs/ BPCDs embedded in the matrix via the covalent bonds in their synthesized processes. XRD patterns of BCD/ BPCD composites treated with different microwave heating times (170 s, 195 s, and 220 s) were also measured to explore the kinds of matrix materials (Figure S12 a-c). With the microwave heating, the (022) diffraction peak of g-C3N4 at 27.8° appears, and the intensity becomes stronger and stronger, the curves increasingly similar to the g-C3N4 prepared from urea in a tube furnace (Figure S12d). Based on the results of FTIR, XRD, and our reports before[41,42], the flake g-C3N4 with a small size was verified to play a crucial role in composing the matrix materials in these composites. Under microwave heating, urea translated to the g-C3N4 and BCDs/ BPCDs embedded into the matrix through covalent bonds, generating a dehydration reaction between function groups in CDs and urea/ g-C3N4. The covalent bonds between CDs and g-C3N4 prevent the aggregation-induced quenching of CDs and reduce the non-radiative transition and promote the ISC process. Notably, after a longer heating time, the flake g-C3N4 could translate to the bulk g-C3N4 with a large size and affect the photoluminescence properties. SEM images of BCD/ BPCD composites synthesized by microwave-heating for 210 s, 225 s, and 300 s were shown in Figure S13a. With the heating time increasing, the nanosheets with a diameter of 1–3 μm (195 s) become larger (210 s) and then translate to the bulk structure with a diameter of more than 10 μm (225 s and 300 s) gradually. In addition, photoluminescence emission spectra of BCD/ BPCD composites with different heating times were also measured and shown in Figure S13b and c. It can be seen the powders prepared for 195 s have the strongest emission intensity, and then the signal decreases with the continuous heating time. Based on the above results, the composite with a small size (diameter of 1–3 μm) nanosheet structure has the strongest emission intensity. Still, photoluminescence becomes weaker when they translate to the bulk structure with larger size after a longer heating time.

The possible mechanism of afterglow color temperature response in BCD/BPCD composites was proposed and shown in Fig. 5 . During the microwave heating treatment, the BCDs/ BPCDs embedded in the g-C3N4 matrix via the covalent bond greatly stabilized the triplet states in CDs and suppressed the vibration dissipation (non-radiative transition), so the composite realized highly efficient singlet and triplet state radiative transition. For the BCD composite, the △EST between S and the T state is sufficiently small to promote the RISC process and generate TADF emission. But at lower temperatures (such as 80 K), non-radiative transition and RISC processes were greatly suppressed. The RTP emission centered at 450 nm (excitons: S1-T-S0) is critical in the afterglow emission. With the temperature increasing, the RTP intensity at 450 nm becomes weaker and weaker due to the stronger non-radiative transition. Because of the thermally activated RISC process, TADF emission (excitons: S1-T-S1-S0) centered at 410 nm increased as the temperature rose and played a key role in the composite afterglow emission at room temperature. For the BPCDs, a new energy state (S2 and T2) was generated via the dropping of P elements, and the △ES1T1 and △ES2T2 were also small enough to promote the RISC process. Under 360 nm excitation, blue and green fluorescence emission could be observed in BPCD composites simultaneously because of the two kinds of singlet state radiative transition (excitons: S2-S0 and S1-S0). At a lower temperature, the non-radiative transition was suppressed obviously, and the cyan afterglow was mainly composed by the RTP emission, which centered at 455 nm and 512 nm (excitons: S2-T2-S0 and S1-T1-S0). With the temperature increasing, the intensity of TADF (excitons: S2-T2-S1-S0 and S1-T1-S1-S0) at 425 nm and 508 nm have a noticeable enhancement via the thermally activated RISC process and RTP emission becomes weaker and weaker. So the color temperature response afterglow was realized based on the dual-mode triplet excitons radiative transition in BCD/ BPCD composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Information encryption and single component delay WLED
ce:section.ce:para: These highly efficient fluorescence/ afterglow emission composites exhibit broad application in data security, and a simple concept of information encryption was first developed based on the BCD composite powders and blue CDs phosphors we have reported before. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the numbers “8888” were written by BCD composites and blue phosphors. Under sunlight and 365 nm UV light irradiation, the pattern of “8888” appeared. However, only the encrypted information of “2023” could be observed when the UV light is off. This simple process demonstrates the great potential of BCD/ BPCD composites in advanced security applications.

The photoluminescence spectrum of BPCD composite powders shows two emission centers. It exhibits extensive full widths at half-maximum values, which have the potential to develop into white phosphors in the lighting fields. As shown in Figure S14, under UV light excitation, the information in the paper could be illuminated by the white photoluminescence from BPCD composite powders in a bottle. Interestingly, when the UV light is off, the afterglow intensity from these powders remains a brightness, and the information also could be observed by eyes still 3 s. In addition, the BPCD composite also exhibits good photoluminescence stability under 365 nm ultraviolet radiation (as shown in Figure S15). Based on the excellent fluorescence/ afterglow dual-mode emission properties, BPCDs composites are applied as a color conversion layer in delay WLED prototypes. Mixing BPCD composite powders with epoxy-silicone resin with specific ratios, then coated on the surface of the Indium gallium nitride (InGaN) microchips which emitted 365 nm light to form WLED. As shown in Fig. 6b, an efficient white emission with a broad spectrum region from 400 to 700 nm could be observed from the fluorescence/ afterglow components when WLED turned on, and the white light characteristics with CIE coordinates, color temperature (CT) and CRI of (0.32, 0.34), 6078 K, and 90.2, respectively (Fig. 6c). Compared with the PL spectra of the BPCD composite, a redshift could be observed in the WLED spectrum, which could be illustrated as the inevitable consecutive reabsorption. The photographs of the delay WLED turned on and off were shown in Fig. 6c, the afterglow light could still be emitted for at least 4 s under no excitation. Based on these results, the BPCD composite WLED exhibits excellent application prospects in eye protection delay lighting, giving eyes a buffer time to reduce transient blindness. In addition, the delay emission properties of WLED also show great potential in producing alternating current LEDs.

In summary, two kinds of CD-based TADF/ RTP dual mode afterglow composites were obtained through embedded BCDs/ BPCDs into the flake g-C3N4 with small size by a simple one-step microwave treatment. At room temperature, the BCD composite exhibit a long-lived deep blue TADF at 410 nm with 270 ms, and the BPCD composite shows dual-color TADF emission at 425 nm and 508 nm with long lifetimes of 250 ms and 648 ms. Because of the competition between RTP and TADF emission under different temperatures, BCD/ BPCD composites exhibit the properties of afterglow color temperature response. A simple concept of information encryption was developed based on the BCD composites and demonstrated the great potential of these composites in advanced security applications. More importantly, BPCD composites exhibit a high overall QY (44 %) and broad FWHM (over 140 nm) at the visible region, the single component fluorescence/ TADF dual-emission delay WLED with CIE coordinates and CRI of (0.32, 0.34), and 92 was developed by integrating BPCD composite in UV-LED chip. This study may motivate CD-based TADF materials applied in advanced lighting, such as designing eyes protection WLED and producing alternating current LED in future applications.

Wenquan Sun: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xin Bao: Methodology, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Hui Wang: Software, Formal analysis. Zixuan Liu: Data curation. Zhen Tian: Methodology. Hui Li: Formal analysis, Supervision. Xi Yuan: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (YDZJ202301ZYTS254, 20210101161JC), the Science and Technology Project of Jilin Province Education Department (JJKH20220424KJ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12004051).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.158655.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
